You cannot only use the ohsome quality API over the Dashboard. With a little bit of python knowledge, you can access all indicators sending your request with a python script.

# Currentness

import geojson
import requests
import geopandas as gpd
import pandas as pd
import time
from concurrent.futures import ThreadPoolExecutor, as_completed
import sys

indicator = "/currentness"
topic = "land-cover"
input_geom_path = "friedberg.geojson"
output_geom_path = "your_output_layer.gpkg"
max_workers = 20

base_url = "https://api.quality.ohsome.org/v1-test"
endpoint = "/indicators"
url = base_url + endpoint + indicator

gdf = gpd.read_file(input_geom_path)
gdf["result_value"] = pd.Series([None] * len(gdf), dtype="float")
gdf["response_time"] = pd.Series([None] * len(gdf), dtype="float")

headers = {"accept": "application/json"}

def fetch(index, geometry):
    bpolys = geojson.Feature(geometry=geometry)
    bpolys_collection = geojson.FeatureCollection([bpolys])

    parameters = {
        "topic": topic,
        "bpolys": bpolys_collection,
    }
    for attempt in range(5):
        try:
            print(f"posting request for index {index}")
            startresponse = time.time()
            response = requests.post(url, headers=headers, json=parameters, timeout=600)
            response.raise_for_status()
            result = response.json()
            endresponse = time.time()
            responsetime = endresponse - startresponse
            value = result["result"][0]["result"]["value"]
            return index, value, responsetime
        except requests.RequestException as e:
            print(f"Attempt {attempt + 1} failed at index {index}: {e}")
            if attempt < 3:
                print("Retrying...")
                time.sleep(2)
            else:
                print("Max retries reached. Skipping.")
                return index, None, None

start = time.time()
with ThreadPoolExecutor(max_workers=max_workers) as executor:
    futures = [executor.submit(fetch, i, gdf.geometry.iloc[i]) for i in range(len(gdf))]

    for future in as_completed(futures):
        index, value, responsetime = future.result()
        gdf.at[index, "result_value"] = value
        gdf.at[index, "response_time"] = responsetime
        print(f"Completed index {index}: {value}")

end = time.time()
print(f"Calculation took {end - start:.2f} seconds")

gdf.to_file(output_geom_path, driver="GPKG")



# Attribute Completeness

import geojson
import requests
import geopandas as gpd
import pandas as pd
import time
from concurrent.futures import ThreadPoolExecutor, as_completed
import sys

indicator = "/attribute-completeness"
topic = "roads"
input_geom_path = "your_bounding_box.geojson"
output_geom_path = "your_output_layer.gpkg"
max_workers = 20



base_url = "https://api.quality.ohsome.org/v1-test"
endpoint = "/indicators"
url = base_url + endpoint + indicator

gdf = gpd.read_file(input_geom_path)
gdf["result_value"] = pd.Series([None] * len(gdf), dtype="float")
gdf["response_time"] = pd.Series([None] * len(gdf), dtype="float")

headers = {"accept": "application/json"}

def fetch(index, geometry):
    bpolys = geojson.Feature(geometry=geometry)
    bpolys_collection = geojson.FeatureCollection([bpolys])

    parameters = {
        "topic": topic,
        "bpolys": bpolys_collection,
        "attributes": ["surface"]
    }
    for attempt in range(5):
        try:
            print(f"posting request for index {index}")
            startresponse = time.time()
            response = requests.post(url, headers=headers, json=parameters, timeout=600)
            response.raise_for_status()
            result = response.json()
            endresponse = time.time()
            responsetime = endresponse - startresponse
            value = result["result"][0]["result"]["value"]
            return index, value, responsetime
        except requests.RequestException as e:
            print(f"Attempt {attempt + 1} failed at index {index}: {e}")
            if attempt < 3:
                print("Retrying...")
                time.sleep(2)
            else:
                print("Max retries reached. Skipping.")
                return index, None, None

start = time.time()
with ThreadPoolExecutor(max_workers=max_workers) as executor:
    futures = [executor.submit(fetch, i, gdf.geometry.iloc[i]) for i in range(len(gdf))]

    for future in as_completed(futures):
        index, value, responsetime = future.result()
        gdf.at[index, "result_value"] = value
        gdf.at[index, "response_time"] = responsetime
        print(f"Completed index {index}: {value}")

end = time.time()
print(f"Calculation took {end - start:.2f} seconds")

gdf.to_file(output_geom_path, driver="GPKG")

The attribute completenss indicator derives the ratio of OSM features that hold one or multiple attributes. Depending on the feature, you can choose from a different set of attributes. If you choose more than one attribute in one querie, the result will be combined and the result figure will show the percentage of all features, that hold all of your chosen attributes.

In this drop down manu you can select one ore multiple attributes.

-> Intrinsic approach

Calculates the percentage of features that contain a certain attribute. If the feature you are investigating is from geometry line, the quota is calculate bades on the length in km. For geometry node, the number of all features is compared with the number of features presenting the selected attribute/s.

You find the results of the query in the result log below.
The green, yellow or red banner on the top left indicates the completeness level.

There are three quality categories:

low completeness < 25%

medium completeness 25% - 75%

high completeness > 75%

The thresholds for the quality categories where selected by viewing the quota distribution of many areas and attributes and must not be seen as an objective metric.

With the building comparison indicator, you can check the ratio of OSM building area compared to two reference datasets.
It is available only for the bilding area feature.

-> Extrinsic approach

The result is the ratio of the total area of buildings in OSM devided by the total area of buildings  in the reference dataset.
According to the availability of the reference dataset in your area of interest, the result log will provide you with one, or both comparison figures.
The building comparison is an extrinsic method, meaning it compares OSM data to non-OSM reference datasets.
This provides and indication for the completeness of OSM data. However, the reference datasets must not be seen as overall accurate and up to date.
The result is the ratio of the total area of buildings in OSM devided by the total area of buildings in the reference dataset.

Reference datasets:

EUBUCCO: EUBUCCO is an europe wide building footprint dataset, derived from administrative datasets. Over 300 million building footprints across europe are covered. This dataset is the most complete dataset for building footprints. However, it is older and could be outdated and it does only cover areas inside of europe.

Microsoft Buildings: The Microsoft Buildings dataset is a worldwide building footprints dataset. A machine learning approach was used to derived building footprints from satellite imagery. Besides the not preventable mistakes that happen with machine learning feature detection, the big advantage of this dataset is the world wide coverage.

In the area of interest panel at the dashboard, you can see white, light grey and dark grey regions, when activating the building comparison indicator. This indicates the availability of the reference datasets.

In white areas, both reference datasets are availabe.

Light grey areas indicate, that only one of the two possiple reference datasets is available.

Dark grey areas are areas without reference datasets.

A result will be calculated if at least 50% of your selected area is covered by a reference dataset.

The following figure shows an example of the building comparison indicator where EUBUCCO covers more area of the selected area of interest than Microsoft Buildings. For the comparison, only OSM building footprints located within the area covered by the reference dataset are considered. As a result, the total OSM building area is presented smaller in the Microsoft Buildings comparison than in the EUBUCCO comparison.
The Currentness Indicator measures how up-to-date OSM elements are by analyzing the distribution of their most recent contributions.

-> Intrinsic approach

The currentness indicator considers all edits to geometry and tags (excluding deletions) since 2008, aggregated in monthly intervals. The result value is calculated as the percentage of elements that were last edited in the last 36 months normalised to a range of 0 to 1. Each contribution is assigned to one of three quality levels:

Outdated (< 0.5): Features that have not been updated for a longer period are labelled as out-of-date. These thresholds are expressed in months since the current date. For each feature class, the relative share of contributions falling into these bins is computed.

In-between (0.5 - 0.75): Contributions that were last edited within an intermediate range are classified as in-between.

Up-to-date (> 0.75): Features are considered up-to-date if
their last edit falls within a predefined short time window.
The land cover completeness indicator calculates the percentage of total area that is covered by OpenStreetMap land cover data.

-> Intrinsic approach

The land cover completeness indicator quantifies the share
of an area of interest (AOI) covered by OSM land cover
data. It is calculated intrinsically as the ratio of the
summed area of OSM land cover polygons to the total area
of the AOI. The result value is calculated as the ratio of
area covered by LULC normalised to a range of 0 to 1:

Low completeness (< 0.5)

Medium completeness (0.5 - 0.85)

High completeness (> 0.85)

This indicator is only available for the land cover and land use filter. This selects all OSM tags that define land cover and land use like build-up areas, forest or inland waters. You can find a detailed list of filters for all features in the 

Topic Definitions
Overlapping OSM land cover polygons will be counted multiple times and falsely improve the land cover completeness ratio.

This indicator evaluates the agreement between OSM land cover data and the CORINE Land Cover (CLC) dataset.
It can be applied either across multiple CLC classes or for selected classes individually.

OSM features are assigned to CLC Level 2 classes based
on their landuse=* and natural=* tags.
The spatial intersection of OSM polygons with CLC
polygons produces composite geometries carrying both the OSM- and CLC-derived class labels. This step ensures
that discrepancies in class definitions can be directly
analyzed at the polygon level. Areas where one of the
datasets is not available are ignored.
For the defined area of interest, polygons resulting from
the intersection of OSM and CLC data are aggregated
into a confusion matrix. From this, we calculate class-
specific and overall accuracy metrics, including precision,
recall, and the F1 score. All metrics are area-weighted,
ensuring that the spatial extent of polygons is taken into
account rather than treating features as equally weighted
units. This approach enables a systematic evaluation of the
thematic consistency of OSM land cover data with respect
to authoritative CLC reference data.
The result value is calculated as the area-weighted F1-
score normalised to a range of 0 to 1. Values below 0.6
are considered low thematic agreement, values between
0.6 and 0.85 as medium agreement and values above 0.85
as high agreement between OSM and CLC.

-> Extrinsic approach.

The OSM features are assigned to CLC Level 2 classes based on their landuse=* and natural=* tags, as shown in the table below. The spatial intersection of OSM polygons with CLC polygons produces composite geometries carrying both the OSM- and CLC-derived class labels. This step ensures that discrepancies in class definitions can be directly analyzed at the polygon level. Areas where one of the
datasets is not available are ignored. For the defined area of interest, polygons resulting from the intersection of OSM and CLC data are aggregated into a confusion matrix. From this, we calculate class- specific and overall accuracy metrics, including precision, recall, and the F1 score. All metrics are area-weighted, ensuring that the spatial extent of polygons is taken into account rather than treating features as equally weighted units. This approach enables a systematic evaluation of the thematic consistency of OSM land cover data with respect to authoritative CLC reference data. The result value is calculated as the area-weighted F1- score normalised to a range of 0 to 1.

Low thematic accuracy (< 0.6)

Medium thematic accuracy (0.6 - 0.85)

High thematic accuracy (> 0.85)

In its current form, the 

CORINE Land Cover (CLC) product offers a pan-European land cover and land use inventory with 44 thematic classes, ranging from broad forested areas to individual vineyards. CORINE uses a 3-level nomenclature for land cover classes.
Here, we use the “CORINE Land Cover 5 ha, Stand 2021 (CLC5-2021)” provided by the German Federal Agency for Cartography and Geodesy and level 2 of the nomenclature (e.g. 1.1 Urban Fabric, 1.2 Industrial, commercial and transport units).

OSM features are assigned a CLC class based on their tags as specified below.
We calculate the intersection of OSM land cover polygons and CORINE land cover polygons.
The resulting polygons contain both the OSM CLC class and the CORINE class.

CLC (level 2)

OSM tags

1.1 Urban Fabric

landuse in (residential, retail)

1.2 	Industrial, commercial and transport units

landuse in (industrial, commercial, port, railway, lock) or leisure in (marina)

1.3 	Mine, dump and construction sites

landuse in (quarry, construction, landfill, brownfield)

1.4 	Artificial, non-agricultural vegetated areas

landuse in (recreation_ground, allotments, village_green, cemetery, grass) or leisure in (park, garden, pitch, golf_course, playground, stadium, recreation_ground, common, dog_park)

2.1 	Arable land

landuse in (greenhouse_horticulture, greenhouse, farmland, farmyard)

2.2 	Permanent crops

landuse in (vineyard, orchard)

2.3 	Pastures

landuse in (meadow)

2.4 	Heterogeneous agricultural areas

For class 2.4 many of the OSM tags from 2.1 apply. Therefore class 2.4 is never assigned.

3.1 	Forests

landuse in (forest) or natural in (wood)

3.2 	Scrub and/or herbaceous vegetation associations

landuse in (greenfield) or natural in (grassland, scrub, heath, fell)

3.3 	Open spaces with little or no vegetation

natural in (beach, scree, shingle, bare_rock, sand, glacier, mud, glacier, rock, cliff, fill)

4.1 	Inland wetlands

natural in (wetland)

4.2 	Maritime wetlands

landuse in (salt_pond)

5.1 	Inland waters

natural in (water, pond) or landuse in (basin, reservoir)

5.2 	Marine waters

Marine waters are not mapped in OSM. Therefore class 5.2 is never assigned.

Schultz, Michael, Janek Voss, Michael Auer, Sarah Carter, and Alexander Zipf. 2017. “Open Land Cover from OpenStreetMap and Remote Sensing.” International Journal of Applied Earth Observation and Geoinformation 63 (May): 206–13. 

Schultz et al. (2017).

The Mapping Saturation Indicator provides (alongside the completeness indicators) a measure of completeness by analyzing how the
growth of mapped features in OSM approaches a natural maximum over time.

-> Intrinsic approach

The central premise of the mapping saturation is that each feature has a finite extent in reality. As mapping activity increases, the number or length of newly added objects per time step decreases, and the cumulative growth curve gradually saturates toward the true value.
To estimate this saturation level, we use the historical evolution of OSM contributions and fit non-linear regression models, to the cumulative growth curve. The workflow for the mapping saturation indicator consists of four main steps.

The temporal evolution of OSM data is extracted for the selected feature class, aggregated at monthly intervals beginning in 2008.

The cumulative time series of mapped features is fitted with different logistic growth models using a non- linear regression approach.

The best-fitting model is selected based on statistical error criteria.

The asymptote of the fitted model is used to approximate the maximum number of mappable objects, and completeness is calculated as the integral of the actual OSM data to the fitted saturation curve over the last 3 years normalised to
a range of 0 to 1.

This results in three quality steps:

Not saturated (<0.3)

Ongoing mapping (0.3 - 0.97)

High saturation (> 0.97)

The mapping saturation result curve can be tricky to inperpret. How meaningful the result value is, also comes down to the shape of the saturation curve.

Here you see an example of a classic ‘good’ saturation curve. Over the years, more and more data was added over time. In the period from 2008 to 2010 the most length was mapped in a short time. Than, slowely less lenght was mapped over time, but a continuous mapping is still visible.

In this example you see a differently shaped saturation curve. The data was added not continously and slowely saturation, but it reaccuring chunks, which makes this staircase optic.

Informativeness varies across topics: performs better for small, discrete objects (e.g., houses) but worse for large polygons (e.g., land use), where a single abrupt mapping step can lead to low completeness.

Gröchenig S et al. (2014): Digging into the history of VGI data-sets: results from a worldwide study on OpenStreetMap mapping activity (

https://​doi​.org​/10​.1080​/17489725​.2014​.978403)

Barrington-Leigh C and Millard-Ball A (2017): The world’s user-generated road map is more than 80% complete (

Barrington-Leigh & Millard-Ball (2017) pmid:28797037)

Josephine Brückner, Moritz Schott, Alexander Zipf, and Sven Lautenbach (2021): Assessing shop completeness in OpenStreetMap for two federal states in Germany (

Brückner et al. (2021))

The road comparison indicator predicts the OSM road data completeness based on a reference dataset. The Result is a ratio of the length of reference roads which are covered by OSM roads to the total length of reference dataset.

-> Extrinsic method

The indicator identifies corresponding road geometries in OSM and the reference dataset to calculate the ratio of matched road length in km.
The Microsoft Roads dataset is used as a reference. However, the reference dataset must not be seen as overall accurate and up to date.
The road comparison indicator is only available for the cars (all highway) feature.

Reference dataset:

Microsoft Roads: The Microsoft Roads dataset is a worldwide dataset of roads. A machine learning approach was used to derived roads from satellite imagery.
The road thematic accuracy shows the alignment of road specific attributes in OSM and Basic DLM.

-> Extrinsic approach.

This indicator is not approved to indicate the quality of the OSM data. It shows the alignment between OSM and a reference dataset without assuming one dataset is superior to the other.

The indicator is only available for the feature roads (cars) and within the boundaries of germany.

You can select one attribute per query.

OSM roads and DLM roads are matched using 

map-matching-2 with a Markov decision process–based model.
For the matched roads first the presence of each attribute in both datasets is checked. If they are present in both,
the values are compared. By default, the values are compared directly, but there are some exceptions:

For the name, the 

Lhevenstein distance was calculated. The names
were counted as a match when their Levensthein ratio was above 0.85.

For surface, OSM tags and DLM tags are matched. All OSM tags that are not in the following table, were counted as not matching.

DLM value

OSM tags

concrete

concrete

bitumen, asphalt

asphalt

pavemed

paving_stones, sett, brick, cobblestone, unhewn_cobblestone

rock, fragmented

fine_gravel, gravel, sand, compacted, pebblestone
For the width, a tolerance of 1 m was applied.
To check the direction of the road, the vector of both geometries is calculated. If both have the same sign it is counted as a match.

The Basic DLM is published by the German Federal Agency for Cartography and Geodesy and describes the topographical
objects of the landscape and the relief of the Earth’s surface in vector format. The objects are defined by their spatial
location, geometric type, descriptive attributes, and relationships to other objects. Each object has a unique
identification number throughout Germany. The roads from this dataset are used for this indicator.

Attribute

OSM tags

DLM attribute

Description

name

name, ref

NAM

The name or reference of a road.

surface

surface

OFM

The material the surface of the road is made of.

lanes

lanes

FSZ

The amount of lanes on this road segment.

width

width

BRF

The width of the road.

oneway

oneway

FAR

The direction of oneway streets.

In this example figure, the alignment of the road attribute ‘name’ was checkt.
The blue columns indicate the attribute present in each dataset (OSM and Basic DLM).
The purple columns indicate the alignment of the two dataset and visualize the similar and different attributes in number and percentage.

A. Wöltche, “Open source map matching with Markov decision processes: A new method and a detailed benchmark with existing approaches”, Transactions in GIS, vol. 27, no. 7, pp. 1959–1991, Oct. 2023, doi: 

Wöltche (2023).
This indicator calculates how many unique users contributed to a specific topic, grouped by months.

-> Intrinsic method

The monthly amount of unique users who edited a specific topic in the area of interest are derived for the entire time range of OSM.
Additionally, the median for the last 3 years are calculated as well as a regression line to see the current tendency of user activity.

The user activity assesses contributor engagement within the study area and is not directly giving information about data quality.
However, combining it with other indicators and viewing absolute numbers and regression trends allow some conclusions about the sustainability of the OSM community.

Welcome to the ohsome Dashboard tutorial! Here you find explanations and user guidlines to get the all the information about the OpenStreetMap (OSM) database you need for any project.

OpenStreetMap is a rich source of user-generated, freely available geospatial data for many applications. However, varying data quality and a lack of standardization can make it challenging to use. We developed the ohsome Dashboard for you to assess OSM data quality globally or for specific areas and helping you determine if it meets your project needs.

The ohsome dashboard enables analysis of OSM data with full version history, no programming required. It generates statistics and visualizes them directly, with customizable filters and groupings by tags, types, region, and time. The ohsome Quality API (OQAPI) provides access to OSM data quality information for specific regions and use cases.

This tutorial was created with collaboration of 

Bundesamt für Kartographie und Geodäsie (BKG).

On this page, you find a simple overview on how to navigate the 

ohsome Dashboard.

On the upper left tab, you can choose between the OSM History Stats and OSM Quality Analyses.
The History Stats gives you an insight into the eventful history of OSM data for any region and time period you are interested in.
The Quality Analyses offer many different qualitiy indicators you can use to validate OSM data for your area of interes.

Topics are defined by a set of OSM Tags and Keys that match a certain object.
At the left window, navigate to OSM Quality Analysis.
Under “Topic” you find a drop down menu. Open it and get familiar with all the topics you can chose from.
Select the topic you want to explore.
The OSM dashboard will automatically create the appropriate OSM filter regarding on the selected topic.

Choose your area of interest on the map on the right window. You can choose along governmental boundaries or self defined bounding boxes. Zoom in to catch a more precice border for your area of interest.
You can run queries for multiple regions at a time. Just click at multiple areas and remove them with another click, or close the blue box under the map.

Here you can analyse OSM data along multiple Quality Dimensions:
Quality Dimensions
Completeness

Mapping Saturation

Attribute Completeness

Building Comparison

Land Cover Completeness

Road Comparison
Currentness

Currentness
Thematic Accuracy

Land Cover Thematic Accuracy

Road Thematic Accuracy
Others

User Activity

Depending on the Topic you chose, you will find the available Quality Indicators to select. Select the once you are interested in and hit the “run query” button to load your figures.
If you are running the Attribute Completeness or Road Thematic Accuracy Indicator, you can select certain attributes. For taking advantage of that, activate the indicator and open the drop-down menu to find the set of attributes you can choose from at your Topic. If you choose multiple attributes in one query, it will be presented as one result.
